straight_beat.name: Delmar Fontenot
straight_beat.column: The Astros lost again. Not in a way that feels inevitable, but in a way that feels like a missed opportunity—a game they should have won, squandered by the same old combination of pitching frailty and defensive lapses. Framber Valdez got the start, and for the first three innings, he looked like the Valdez who once made us believe in the idea of a 200-inning season. He was sharp, commanding, and throwing strikes with the kind of precision that made you think, *This is why we paid for the ticket*. But then came the fourth inning, and the Mariners’ lineup, which

optimist.name: Luz Barquero
optimist.column:
The Astros lost yesterday, but let’s not pretend that’s the end of the story. No, that’s just the game clock winding down before the real work begins. You know what I’m talking about—those late-inning rallies, the kind that make you slap the steering wheel when the crowd’s roaring. We’re not talking about a loss here; we’re talking about a temporary detour on the road to something bigger.  

Look, the record’s 6-10, and yeah, that’s a long ways from the playoff picture we all dreamed about. But here’s the thing: this team hasn’t quit. Not once. Not even after that 6-1 loss to Seattle. You saw the way the bullpen held serve, the way Bregman kept grinding at the plate like he’s got a personal vendetta against the Mariners’ pitching staff. Losing streak? Nah, that’s just the boys stretching. Bregman’s swing is one at-bat away from April flipping into June.  

And let’s not forget the young guys in the rotation. They’re still finding their rhythm, and that’s

pessimist.name: Coop Yarbrough
pessimist.column:
The Astros lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that makes you want to rip the scoreboard off the wall and throw it into the bayou. Six runs to one, a record that’s now 6-10, and a gap that’s wider than a moonlit highway. You’d think after all this time, the team would’ve learned how to win. But no, they’re still here, still stumbling, still clinging to the hope that maybe, just maybe, the universe will hand them a break they don’t deserve.  

It’s not the pitching, not really. Not anymore. The starters are still the same guys who’ve been here since the 2017 asterisk started glowing like a neon sign in a dark alley. They’re still throwing strikes, still getting hit hard, still leaving runners on base like they’re trying to teach the game a lesson. The bullpen? Oh,
